The rest of the prophecy regards his successors. Cyrus waged war upon them, and Baltassar was slain in a conspiracy. See Isaias XLVII., and Jeremias L.
At last the sword fell upon Babylon itself.

22:2.text: Blood. Why dost thou cease to admonish and rebuke the people?
Pronounce sentence on Jerusalem, (Haydock) for her cruelties.

22:6.text: Hath. Septuagint, "was mixed with his relations," ver. 10., etc., (Haydock) fighting against them.

22:8.text: Sabbaths, by servile works, and by their sinful deportment.

22:9.text: Slanderers, hateful to all, Leviticus 19:16., and Proverbs 11:13.

22:10.text: Father, his widow, their mother-in-law, or one of his wives.

22:12.text: Me. "The remembrance of God excludes all sins."

22:13.text: God wishes the sinner's salvation, Genesis 6:6.

22:15.text: In thee. I will purify thee by violent medicines.

22:16.text: Possess, "as an inheritance:" (Septuagint; Haydock) "I will defile:" (Aquila) "wound thee." (Symmachus) Hebrew is very different: (Calmet) "thou hast inherited in thyself," (Haydock) being no longer willing to be subject.

22:18.text: Dross. Hebrew, "a mixture." Septuagint, "God will purify the silver," Isaias 4:4.

22:24.text: Rained upon, or watered with the instructions of the prophets, Ezechiel 20:46.

22:25.text: Conspiracy. The false prophets defend each other, (Calmet) while the priests connive at their proceedings, (Haydock) and set the example of wickedness.

22:28.text: Without. Septuagint, "they shall fall, seeing," etc.

22:30.text: The prayers of the just are very powerful; and God wishes them to exert themselves for sinners.

23:3.text: Down. Virgins used the stomacher, or (Haydock) fascia pectoralis, Isaias 3:24. This chapter resembles the 16th.

23:4.text: Oolla and Ooliba. God calls the kingdom of Israel Oolla, which signifies their own habitation, because they separated themselves from his temple; and the kingdom of Juda Ooliba, which signifies his habitation in her, because of his temple among them in Jerusalem.

The ten tribes first gave way to idolatry, and were more numerous. (Calmet) -- In Egypt the people were united, yet abandoned to idolatry in their youth, when they were only beginning to increase. Afterwards the ten tribes set up altars for themselves, at Dan and Bethel.

23:5.text: Assyrians. That is, the idols of the Assyrians: for all that is said in this chapter of the fornications of Israel and Juda, is to be understood in a spiritual sense of their disloyalty to the Lord, by worshipping strange gods.
The Assyrians taught the worship of Baal, the high places, etc. The Egyptians also promoted their abominations.

23:6.text: Blue, or purple. This was reserved for the principal nobility.

23:8.text: Egypt. They always retained an affection for those idols, the calf, Adonis, etc.

23:10.text: Women. Having once lost all sense of decorum, they became more abandoned.

23:14.text: Colours. She was enamoured at the account or picture of these people, without ever having seen them.

23:16.text: Messengers. Achaz invited the Assyrians, and imitated their altars, etc., 4 Kings 16:2., etc.
The Chaldeans afterwards possessed the country.

23:17.text: Love. Literally, "of breasts," as Hebrew also signifies. Here it denotes the temples.

23:19.text: Remembering, or "causing to be remembered" by God, who seemed to have forgotten those ancient scenes of wickedness, Ezechiel 21:24.

23:20.text: Asses. He means the Egyptians, (chap. 16:26.) in whom the kings of Juda trusted.

23:22.text: About. The Philistines, Ammonites, etc., shall join the Chaldeans, (Haydock) and Egypt shall desert the Jews in their greatest need.

23:23.text: Nobles. Protestants, "Pekod, and Shoah, and Roa:" (Haydock) which Junius takes to be the three great divisions of the empire. Others suppose they denote the dignities, though they occur not in Daniel.
Captains. Hebrew, "Phachat, and Saganim, (ver. 6, 12.) and Schalischim;" officers mentioned [in] Exodus 14:7., and 2 Kings 23:8.
Renowned, called to the assemblies and councils of the prince, Numbers 1:16.
Horsemen. Protestants, "all of them riding upon horses."

23:25.text: Jealousy, the most violent of passions.
So the Chaldeans are styled, as Assur is the rod, Isaias 10:5.
Ears, the punishment of adulteresses: both king and priests suffer.
Thus the captives were probably treated. (Calmet) See Seneca, Ira. 3:20.

23:26.text: Glory; vanity, or of the temple, Ezechiel 16:17., and Osee 2:4.

23:31.text: Cup. Thou shalt be punished like Samaria, dreadfully, 4 Kings 17:4.

23:34.text: Fragments, as drunkards sometimes bite (Haydock) the cup in their rage.
Rend. What had been the occasion of thy fall: (Calmet) pectori planxi.

23:36.text: Deeds. He is thus admonished to condemn them; (chap. 20:4.) as times and places tended to aggravate their crimes, ver. 39.

23:41.text: Bed: it seems at table, Amos 2:8., and Tobias 2:3.
Ointment; using them for thyself, or presenting them to idols, Exodus 30:23, 33.

23:42.text: And that. Protestants, "Sabeans (marginal note and Septuagint, drunkards) from," etc. Any were admitted (Haydock) to the priesthood and to her favours, even the most rustic and deformed, 3 Kings 12:32.

23:45.text: Just, compared with the Jews, whom they shall chastise.

23:47.text: Stones, like adulteresses, Ezechiel 16:38., and Leviticus 20:10.

24:1.text: Year of the World 3414, Year before Christ 590.
Month; January 30, the year of the world 3414.

Ezechiel was then in Mesopotamia, and when the news of the siege commencing on that very day, should arrive; it would make a great impression upon the people, so that they would have confidence in him, (Calmet) as the certainty of the prediction would appear, 4 Kings 25:1.

24:2.text: Pot, to denote Jerusalem: the flesh boiled and consumed in the fire with the bones, would shew the future dismal condition of its chiefs and inhabitants.
The hardened Jews turned such things to ridicule, Ezechiel 11:3.

24:4.text: Choice. Hebrew, "the choice of the bones," or the finest pieces separated from the bones, as the Septuagint and the sequel seem to require.
The bones might serve to burn, ver. 5. (Haydock) See St. Jerome.

24:5.text: The, etc. Literally, "its boiling has grown hot;" the citizens suffer terribly.
Bones. Hebrew hatsamim, (Haydock) may denote the more solid meat.

24:6.text: Rust: the inveterate malice of the city, ver. 12.
Upon it. Hurl the pieces of meat out of the pot, without any choice.
All the people shall feel my indignation, the rich as well as the poor, ver. 13.

24:7.text: She hath. Septuagint, "I have let it corrupt upon. I have not," etc., (ver. 8.; Haydock) as if God spoke. The Jews had committed murder without fear. They had naturally a horror for blood, and the law ordered even that of beasts to be covered, Leviticus 17:13. Now innocent blood cries for vengeance, Genesis 4:10. The punishment shall be as visible as the crime, ver. 8.

24:10.text: And the. Hebrew, "put in the seasoning and the bones," etc.

24:13.text: Thy. Hebrew, "In thy crime there is design," or malice. This rendered the Jews so hateful (Calmet) and irreclaimable.
God had given them abundant instructions (Calmet) and graces; but all was contemned.
When the fire of tribulation does not amend sinners, they are sentenced to hell fire.

24:16.text: Stroke; pestilence, or sudden death. This would make the loss of a dear wife still more afflicting. Yet such distress will fall upon the whole nation, (Calmet) and misery shall increase so much, that a private loss will be almost forgotten.
When a loss is foreseen, it is more easily borne. Private calamities sink in public ones.

24:17.text: Silence, for such manifold calamities, if thou canst screen thyself from the enemy, who will otherwise take offence, as he has brought them on.

Dead. Priests were allowed to mourn only for father or mother, and their unmarried brothers and sisters, Leviticus 21:1. Ezechiel (xliv. 25.) adds, Son and daughter. Many think the wife must also be understood, as she is nearer than a brother. The reasons for these prohibitions did not then subsist, as no sacrifice could be offered in Chaldea; and therefore God here specifies what the prophet was not to do, (Calmet) though lawful on other occasions.

Tire. Literally, "crown," bandage, (Calmet) or parchment, on which parts of the law were written. Septuagint, "Let (Roman edition adds, not) the hair of thy head be curled (or ruffed; sumpeplegmenon) upon thee."
It was usually cut in mourning.
Feet. They were bare, at funerals, and in times of sorrow, 2 Kings 15:30.
Face, like David. Hebrew, "the upper lip," which mourners and lepers covered, Leviticus 13:45.
Mourners. Feasts were prepared by the relations, (Josephus, Jewish Wars 2:1.) and friends sent some food, but no delicacies, to those who mourned, Leviticus 5:9.

24:21.text: Profane, or esteem it no more, (Haydock) but abandon it to the Gentiles.
Feareth to lose; or on which it rests, ver. 25.

24:27.text: No more, if thou darest to speak before the Chaldeans, ver. 17. Reserve thy tears and lamentations for that time.

25:2.text: Ammon. These nations were punished five years after the Jews, Ezechiel 21:19., and Jeremias 27:3.
"Egypt, Syria, Phoenicia, and Arabia," were conquered.
Hast. God saw their dispositions, which they manifested afterwards.
They rejoiced most, and were therefore punished with other nations.

25:3.text: Year of the World 3417, Year before Christ 387.; Jeremias xlix.
Ammon. These nations were punished five years after the Jews, Ezechiel 21:19., and Jeremias 27:3.
"Egypt, Syria, Phoenicia, and Arabia," were conquered.
Hast. God saw their dispositions, which they manifested afterwards.
They rejoiced most, and were therefore punished with other nations.

25:4.text: East; the Chaldeans, (Sanctius) who conquered them, (Haydock) though the Scenite Arabs, who occupied the country after the inhabitants were removed, or the Armenians and Agarens, are rather meant than the Chaldeans.

25:5.text: Rabbath, the capital city of the Ammonites: it was afterwards called Philadelphia.
Flocks. These constituted the riches of those Arabians.

25:7.text: Lord, and that it was not through impotence that my people became a prey. Ammon and Moab returned after some time, ver. 10., and Ezechiel 16:53., and Jeremias 49:6.

25:8.text: Nations, whose gods could not defend them.

25:9.text: Shoulder; reduce to slavery, (Haydock) or take the strong places, (Jeremias 48:7.; Calmet) or cities and strength of Moab.

25:10.text: With, or "like thee." Moab (ver. 11.) shall be treated like Ammon.

25:12.text: Revenge; exhorting the Chaldeans to destroy utterly, (Psalm 136:7.) and seizing every opportunity of injuring the Jews, 2 Paralipomenon 28:18., and Amos 1:11.

25:13.text: South. Hebrew, "Theman," a city at one extremity. The Chaldeans laid waste the country, (chap. 32:29.) but did not remove the people, who seized the southern parts of Juda, till they were forced to submit to the Machabees.

25:15.text: Enmities. They also watched every moment to injure the Jews, Amos 1:5. But Nabuchodonosor was irritated, because they had assisted Tyre, Jeremias 47:4.

25:16.text: Killers. Hebrew, "Ceretheans." David's guards were of this nation, 2 Kings 8:18. They came originally from Crete; and the Septuagint have here, "Cretans;" (Calmet) as Grabe substitutes for "Judges of Sidon."
Remnant. They deserve not the name of a nation, having been much reduced by Egypt and the Chaldeans.

26:1.text: He still dates from the transmigration of Joachin, Ezechiel 1.

Some think he speaks of the first, fourth, or fifth month. Tyre was not besieged till after the ninth day of the fourth month, when Jerusalem was taken; nor could she express her joy for that event before, unless God allude to her dispositions, etc., Ezechiel 25:1.

26:2.text: Gates: places of resort and commerce. The Jews came to Jerusalem frequently from all parts, which increased her beauty and trade. New Tyre expects that more will come to her.

26:3.src: Antiquities 10:11., and contra Apion i.; Calmet
26:3.text: Up. Nabuchodonosor besieged the city for thirteen years. The profane historians read by St. Jerome took no notice of this; but Josephus quotes several.

26:4.text: Dust. She shall be demolished, and the rubbish thrown into the sea, to make a road by which New Tyre in the island might be attacked, ver. 12.

26:5.text: Sea. St. Jerome explains this of New Tyre; Marsham of the Old. To reconcile the different texts, we only need to suppose that both cities were connected by a road thrown up in the sea by Hiram, and repaired by Nabuchodonosor with great labour, (chap. 29:18.) after it had been destroyed by the inhabitants of New Tyre, when they saw the old city on the continent fall a prey.

26:7.text: Kings: Nabuchodonosor (4 Kings 25:28.) or Alexander [the Great], who took Tyre.

26:8.text: Daughters. Many towns were subject to Tyre: almost all Phoenicia acknowledged her dominion, as well as (Calmet) the seas to which her fleets went, ver. 15.
These smaller cities shall fall, and the town shall be of no service except to dry nets.

26:9.text: A covert was thus made for the soldiers, (Veget. 4:15.) when they approached the walls.

26:10.text: Destroyed. Old Tyre was taken by storm. It is doubtful whether it was pillaged, Ezechiel 29:18.

26:11.text: Statues. The citizens chained the golden statue of Apollo to the altar of Hercules, for fear of its leaving them, when Alexander [the Great] attacked the town.
Hiram placed a pillar of gold in the temple of Hercules.

Herodotus (II. 44.) saw another also of emerald stone, (smaragdon) which illuminated the temple in the night. On such the Tyrians might depend; though some render, "the substance or guard of thy strength," denoting the soldiers (Calmet) and towers.
The gods were treated like the people, and their precious ornaments plundered.

26:14.text: More, for seventy years, Isaias 23:15. The people returned at the same time as the Jews. (The year of the world 3468.) Soon after, Zacharias (Zacharias 9.) speaks of Tyre as then subsisting. It was very strong in Alexander's time, (who took it with difficulty, as Antigonus did eighteen years later) and had a very extensive commerce when St. Jerome wrote. But all this must be understood of New Tyre. The old city never regained much splendour.
It is still in ruins. A modern traveller was struck with the completion of this prophecy, beholding a few miserable fishermen drying their nets on the spot!

26:16.text: Sea: colonies, or tributary to Tyre, ver. 8.
Leptis, Utica, Carthage,and Cadiz, were founded by Tyrians.
Some pretend that these cities were attacked by the conquerors, for manifesting their grief. See Josephus, Antiquities x.; Pineda, etc.
But we shall not here follow conjectures.

26:17.text: Dwellest in. Hebrew, "of the seas." Protestants, "seafaring men," (Haydock) being near the sea, or thence deriving thy riches.

26:19.text: Waters; great armies, (ver. 3.) or when thou art in the regions below, Job 26:5.
Tyre was humbled for her pride, but restored after seventy years, Isaias xxiii. Our Saviour retired into those parts, Matthew 15:21.

26:20.text: Everlasting: in the grave, till the day of judgment, Psalm 48:12., and Wisdom 12:5.
Living, assigned to Israel, (chap. 32:24.; Calmet) where holy people adore the true God, and shall rise to life eternal.

26:21.text: For ever: for a long time, (Theodoret) not at all in thy ancient glory.
The city subsisted after the days of Nabuchodonosor and of Alexander [the Great], (Calmet) ver. 14.
But the ancient city was reduced to a mere nothing.

27:1.text: Year of the World 3416, Year before Christ 588.

27:2.text: Lamentation. Such canticles were usual, and very poetical.

27:3.text: Entry, whence merchants may proceed from an excellent harbour to any place.

27:4.text: The description of the Tyrian grandeur, shews their more woeful ruin.

27:5.text: St. Jerome has divided (Calmet) leuthim, "decks of the sea," as yam denotes the sea.

27:6.text: All distant places were styled islands, (Haydock) when they went by water to them.

27:7.text: Linen. Cotton, (Exodus 25:4.) used for standards. Septuagint, "for bed coverlets," or for sails.
Mast. Cleopatra and Caligula were still more sumptuous in their sails.
Elisa, or Elis, famous for purple: yet Tyre was more so.

27:8.text: Aradians. Sidon and Arad were then subject to Tyre, and supplied rowers.
Pilots. They studied no other science.

27:9.text: Gebal. Septuagint, "Biblos," which is the same, 3 Kings 5:18.
Furnished. Hebrew, "were in thee to repair thy breaches." Septuagint, "strengthened thy designs."

27:10.text: Lybians. Hebrew, "Phut." They had been expelled by the Cyreneans. Tyre had in her pay the most warlike nations of Persia, etc. Cyrus soon after shook off the yoke of the Medes, and conquered the Lydians.
Hung up, ver. 11. This was very usual, Canticle of Canticles 4:4., and Isaias 22:8.

27:11.text: The Pygmeans. That is, strong and valiant men. In Hebrew Gammadim.

He does not speak of those fabulous men hardly a cubit high. Gomed signifying a "cubit," has caused them to be styled so here. Septuagint, "guards;" or Symmachus, "Medes." Ezechiel (xxxviii. 6.) speaks of the Gomerim.

27:12.text: Carthaginians. Hebrew, "Tharsis," in Cilicia; (Genesis 10:4.; Calmet) or distant merchants, who came by sea.

27:13.text: Slaves. Those from Greece were much esteemed.
Alas! thirty thousand Tyrians were themselves thus sold by Alexander [the Great]!

27:14.text: Horses. Those of Sarmatia (Calmet) were in high repute. (Pliny, [Natural History?] 8:42.

27:15.text: Dedan. Septuagint, "Rhodians;" or rather Arabs are meant, ver. 20. They might receive ivory from Ethiopia.
Teeth. Hebrew, "horns or tusks," which the elephant casts every year. The ivory is less brittle, 3 Kings 10:18.
Ebony; a hard black wood, like horn.

27:16.text: Syrian: always much addicted to commerce.
Septuagint read Adam for Aram, as if the traffic in men was meant: (Calmet) "ivory, and to those who brought, thou gavest thy rewards. (16) Men of thy traffic," etc.
Linen. Hebrew buts, "silk" extracted from the pinna fish, 1 Paralipomenon 15:27. Silk. Hebrew ramoth, may rather denote unicorns, Job 28:18.
Chodchod. It is the Hebrew name for some precious stone, but of what kind in particular, interpreters are not agreed.
Some say the carbuncle, etc. St. Jerome renders it the jasper, Isaias 54:12.
Here he confesses he knows not the meaning.

27:17.text: Rosin. Our version generally renders this, balm.
It was much used to heal, Jeremias 8:22., and Genesis 37:25.

27:18.text: Rich. Hebrew Chelbon; perhaps the city Chelba, Judges 1:31. The kings of Persia used this wine, and planted vines at Damascus on purpose.

27:19.text: Dan: the citizens of Peneas, the tribe of Dan was in captivity. Grotius places these nations in Zeilan, (Calmet) or Ceylon.

27:20.text: Seats, such as the Turks still use, or to throw over horses instead of saddles.

27:23.text: Haran, or Charae, famous for the residence of Abraham and the defeat of Crassus.
Eden, the province where Paradise was situated.

27:24.text: Cords, in boxes, which had then no locks.

27:25.text: Sea. Hebrew Tharsis, in Cilicia; or large, and fit for long voyages. Thine were the best.

27:26.text: South. Hebrew kodim, (Haydock) "eastern," or rather "burning," here means Nabuchodonosor, who came from the north, (chap. 26:7.; Calmet) or east. The fall of Tyre is described as a shipwreck.

27:30.text: Ashes. They followed the same customs as the Jews.
The latter were ordered to avoid cutting the hair, like them; yet did so, Deuteronomy xiv., and Isaias 22:22.

28:2.text: Prince. The Jews say Hiram, (St. Jerome) supposing that he lived a thousand years; though this is not requisite, as he died only four hundred and thirty years before. The idea is childish. The king who was taken and slain at this siege, was probably (Calmet) Ithobalos.

Origen applies this to the angel guardian of Tyre. St. Augustine thins an allusion is made to the fall of Lucifer. But it may all refer to the king, (Calmet) though others explain part of him and the rest of the devil.

28:3.text: Than Daniel; viz., in thy own conceit. The wisdom of Daniel was so much celebrated in his days, that it became a proverb among the Chaldeans, when any one would express an extraordinary wisdom, to say he was as wise as Daniel.
He was now at court, and had explained the dream of Nabuchodonosor, Daniel 2:27.

28:10.text: Uncircumcised. The Jews deem this a disgrace, 1 Kings 31:4. The king would be exposed to eternal death, being devoid of faith, etc.

28:12.text: Resemblance. The king of Tyre, by his dignity and his natural perfections, bore in himself a certain resemblance to God, by reason of which he might be called the seal of resemblance, etc. But what is here said to him is commonly understood of Lucifer, the king over all the children of pride.
A seal is perfect when it represents things exactly. The prophet speaks ironically, to repress the king's vanity.

28:13.text: Paradise. Thou hadst every thing delightful at Tyre.
Covering. Hebrew, "bandage;" diadem, (Haydock) or belt. Septuagint specify twelve stones. See Exodus 28:17.
Pipes; music used at the coronation, which was celebrated as a birth-day.
God gives and withdraws power from all kings. As long as they act well, they are in a sort of sanctuary.

28:14.text: Stretched out. That is, thy wings extended. This alludes to the figure of the cherubims in the sanctuary, which with stretched out wings covered the ark, (Challoner) on the holy mountain.
Fire. That is, bright and precious stones, which sparkle like fire.
With these the king's robes glittered, or he walked upon them, Esther 1:6.

28:15.text: Iniquity. Tyre was before wicked; but when the king pretended to be a god, (ver. 2.) it could no longer be endured.

28:17.text: Beauty. Riches have corrupted thy heart.

28:18.text: Sanctuaries, or temples, which was a crime (Calmet) in an idolater, as he took them to be the temples of a real deity.

28:22.text: Sidon. It gave rise to Tyre, yet was now inferior, though independent of it, (Isaias 23:4.) or subject to Egypt.

28:24.text: Bitterness. Jezabel came hence; (4 Kings 16:31.) and the Phoenicians hemmed in the Israelites as much as possible, and fought against them.
Sidon was near Tyre, and imitating her crimes was also punished.

28:25.text: When. All shall praise God, seeing that he receives his people again after he has justly chastised them.

29:1.text: Year of the World 3415, Year before Christ 589.

Eleventh. Hebrew, "twelfth." Septuagint, "first of the twelfth month of the twelfth year." There are other variations in the versions. St. Jerome reads the first in Hebrew, as Theodoret does, who says that it and the Syriac have the twelfth year: which is true, if we neglect the points.
The prophets do not observe the order of times. What is here delivered, was sooner fulfilled; or Tyre and Sodom lay nearer than Egypt.
The three next chapters regard that country.

29:2.src: The year of the world 3433.; Calmet
29:2.text: Pharao, Ephree, Jeremias xliv 30. He came to assist Sedecias; but the Chaldeans raised the siege, went to meet him, an defeated his army. After they had subdued the neighbouring nations, Tyre, etc., they fell upon Egypt.

29:3.text: Dragon. Hebrew tannin, (Haydock) whence thunnus may be derived, means any water monster, and seems here put for the crocodile, (Calmet) which Pharao signifies.
It was the symbol of Egypt, (Calmet) and adored by the people, Jeremias 15:2.
Rivers; the different branches of the Nile, and the canals.
Myself. I owe my power to no other.
"Apries is said to think that no god could deprive him of the kingdom, so well he seemed to have established it."
So the ancient Pharao said; I know not the Lord, Exodus 5:2. He boasts of having conducted the waters of the Nile through the land, ver. 9.
This river was honoured as the greatest of the gods.
Terra suis contenta bonis non indiga mercis Aut Jovis; in solo tanta est fiducia Nilo.

29:4.text: Bridle. The Tentyrians jump upon the crocodile's back, give it a club to bite at, which they seize with both hands, and bring it to the shore.

Others throw a hook baited with swine's flesh, and holding the rope on the shore, make a little pig squeak, which draws the attention of the crocodile; and, as it comes for its prey, it swallows the hook, and its eyes being filled with dust is easily slain.
Apries sent an army against Cyrene, which being defeated as it was thought by the king's fault, many of the Egyptians revolted. He sent Amasis to reduce them, but they gave him the crown.

Nabuchodonsor taking advantage of these disturbances, and perhaps invited by Amasis, entered Egypt, drove Apries into Higher Egypt, slew many of the inhabitants, and Jews, etc., and left Amasis to govern the wretched remains of that kingdom. (Usher, the year of the world 3430.) The Scripture, however, seems to say that Pharao was slain; (Jeremias 43.; etc.) (Calmet) which Ctesias assures us was done by Amasis, though Herodotus (II. 169.) says he was killed by the people, and buried with his fathers. This latter circumstance is not very probable: but the historian followed the account of the priests, who would mention what was most honourable for the nation. He seems to have been left unburied, ver. 5. Scales. The people depended on the king and shared his fate.

29:6.text: He promised more than he was able or strove to perform, though he made a show of giving aid.

29:7.text: Loins. They fell upon thee, and thou didst wound (Calmet) or "dissolve" their loins.

29:10.text: Tower; or rather (Calmet) Hebrew and Septuagint, "from Magdol to Syene."
This was on the frontiers of Ethiopia, below the cataracts.

29:11.text: Years, till the third of Cyrus, who gave liberty to all the captives at the beginning of his reign, ver. 13.
Amasis reigned forty-four years in Lower Egypt, (Herodotus 3:10.) over the few whom Nabuchodonosor spared.

29:14.text: Low. The Jews were not more tempted to apply to them for aid.
Amasis strove to shake off the yoke: but Cambyses came and slew many. Psammenites killed himself; (Herodotus 3:9.) or was taken to Susa, and the country laid waste.

Egypt has almost ever since been subject to foreign princes, (Haydock) Persians, Greeks, Romans, Saracens, Mamelukes, and Turks. The trade of Egypt, by caravans, was in a manner destroyed for forty years by Nabuchodonosor, whose victories Megesthenes and Berosus attest 300 years before Christ.

29:17.text: Year of the World 3432, Year before Christ 572.
Year: fifteen (Calmet) or seventeen years after the preceding prophecy, (ver. 1.; Worthington) but on the same subject.

29:18.text: Peeled, with carrying machines of war and burdens for thirteen years.

No reward worth the labour. The new city surrendered upon terms, (Calmet) or the citizens shipped off their most valuable goods, and retired to Carthage, etc. God gives a temporal reward for moral virtues, (St. Jerome) even to infidels.

29:18.src: St. Augustine, City of God 5:12.; and St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologiae] 1:2. q. 114. a. 10.
29:18.text: Thus he rewarded the ancient Romans, and the midwives.
They had no intention of pleasing God, (Calmet) or of directing their labours for his service.

29:21.text: Horn: Zorobabel; (St. Jerome) Jechonias, who was honoured by Evilmerodac; (Tirinus) or Daniel and Mardochai, with all the nation.
Month. Thou shalt speak boldly, and they will give credit to thee henceforward.

30:2.text: Day of vengeance, (Calmet) when the Lord shall judge, (1 Corinthians 5:2.) and Egypt shall fall a prey to the most powerful nation of the Chaldeans.

30:4.text: Ethiopia. Hebrew, "Chus," denoting part of Arabia, or rather Ethiopia.

30:5.text: Covenant: the Jews or auxiliaries. Septuagint add, "the Persians and Cretans."
There were Persians in Africa who followed Hercules.

30:9.text: The Nile was navigable as far as Syene.

30:12.text: Dry. The canals were obstructed (Calmet) which rendered cavalry useless.

30:13.text: Memphis, the capital of Lower Egypt, (Calmet) where the idol or bull (Haydock) Apis was fed.
The Chaldeans made war on the gods as well as on the people.
Prince Amasis was tributary to Babylon. Some of this probably regards the times of Cambyses and Ochus. (Usher, the year of the world 3478 and 3653.) (Calmet) See Ezechiel 29:14.

30:14.text: Taphnis, or Tanis, famous for the miracles of Moses.
Alexandria. In the Hebrew No, which was the ancient name of that city, which was afterwards rebuilt by Alexander the Great, and from his name called Alexandria.
Septuagint, "Memphis or Diospolis;" (Calmet) or Thebes, capital of Higher Egypt; (Bochart) though it seems rather a maritime town, Nahum 3:8.

30:15.text: Pelusium. Hebrew Sin, (Haydock) "muddy," has the same import as pelos.
The place is now called Damietta.

30:18.text: Darkened: war and misery shall ensue.
Pride; overflowing of the Nile. See Jeremias 49:19.

30:20.text: Year, in which Jerusalem was taken. Pharao lost a battle. His country was afterwards invaded, (Calmet) and his subjects rebelled, ver. 21.
Part of the kingdom had been already subdued, 4 Kings 24:7.

31:1.text: Year, about a month before the fall of Jerusalem.

31:3.text: Assyrian. The ruin of this great empire (Calmet) might have admonished the king of Egypt of his frail condition.

31:3.src: Usher, the year of the world 3378.
31:3.text: About thirty-eight years before (Calmet) Ninive had been taken, and its king (Sarac or Chinaladan) slain by his own general, Nabopolassar, and by Astyages, of Media. They divided the empire between them, and the father of Nabuchodonosor fixed his residence at Babylon.
Egypt's monarch thought himself invincible; yet would fall like the Assyrian.

31:4.text: Roots. Various nations paid tribute to the Assyrian, (St. Jerome; Calmet) while he sent his troops, like rivulets, to keep all in subjection.

31:11.text: I have delivered. Here the time past is put for the future; i.e., I shall deliver.
The mighty one, etc., viz., Nabuchodonosor, who conquered both the Assyrians and Egyptians; (Challoner) or rather his father, Nabopolassar, subdued the former, ver. 3.

31:13.text: Branches. The nations continued, but submitted to another master.

31:14.text: Pit. The new king would appoint fresh governors.

31:15.text: Waters, as if they bewailed his fate.
Those whom the king of Ninive had exalted, and the people, who wished not to submit to a foreigner, would no doubt lament the slaughtered monarch, now silent (Haydock) in the grave.

31:16.text: Comforted. The ghosts of princes who had been subject to Serac, seeing his fall, bore their own misfortune with greater content. In the grave there is no distinction of master and slave.
Surviving princes expected some emolument from the change.

31:17.text: Arm; those in power. Septuagint, "his seed." Hebrew Zora, (Haydock) means both.

31:18.text: Famous king of Assyria, or of Egypt.
Mutato nomine de te Fabula narratur.
Though Egypt be like the most potent kingdoms, it shall likewise fall.

32:1.text: Year of the World 3417, Year before Christ 587.
Twelfth. He counts from the captivity of Jechonias, as Sedecias reigned only eleven years.

32:2.text: Dragon, or crocodile; two of the most terrible creatures.
With the horn is not expressed in Hebrew and the crocodile has nothing like a horn. It has four feet, with which it makes the water muddy.

32:3.text: Some take the crocodile with a net, ver. 3.

32:5.text: Corruption. Septuagint, "blood." But romuth (Haydock) means rather "worms."

32:7.text: Out, like a candle, by death; extinctus.
The glory of Egypt was so great, that at its fall the light of heaven seemed diminished.
When, etc., is not in some Latin copies, nor in Hebrew, etc.

32:8.text: Out, like a candle, by death; extinctus.
The glory of Egypt was so great, that at its fall the light of heaven seemed diminished.
When, etc., is not in some Latin copies, nor in Hebrew, etc.

32:9.text: Anger, as they will not know why I have treated thee so severely: or rather, they shall be afraid for themselves, ver. 10.

32:12.text: Invincible, when they wield God's sword. Cyrus easily conquered them.

32:14.text: Oil. It is very transparent. It seems the Nile was rendered muddy by cattle, etc.

32:15.text: Fulness, or all of it shall be desolate.

32:18.text: Apries was slain by order of Amasis.

32:21.text: Most. Septuagint, "giants shall say to thee: Remain in the depth of the pit. Whom dost thou excel? descend," etc.
They vary much in this chapter from the Hebrew.
Helpers; Lybians, etc., Ezechiel 30:5. These shall come to compliment the king.
After a battle, those of the same nation were buried together, ver. 22.
The Egyptians had tombs like houses, in which there were separate holes or apartments (Calmet) as in large vaults.

32:24.text: Shame. They are buried without any distinction.

32:27.text: Not. Some copies of Septuagint omit the negation. Others render the Hebrew, "Have they not slept?" etc. These nations were deprived of military honours, dying like cowards; and therefore their swords were not placed with them in the grave.

It was customary to inter such things as the deceased had liked the most. (Serv. in Virgil's Aeneid X. Arma quibus laetatus habe tua, etc. Simon (1 Machabees 13:29.) placed arms and representations of ships on the pillars at Modin, in honour of his kindred. If Elam, etc., had not received such distinction, why should the Egyptian repine? Were they any better?
The country and king of the Elamites, Assyrians, and other infidels, shall be destroyed.

32:28.text: Midst. This threat would make great impression on the Egyptians, who were particularly solicitous to be buried with their fathers.

32:29.text: Edom. Septuagint add, "and all the Assyrian princes." Some copies omit Edom.
This nation had laid aside circumcision, which Hircan forced them to resume. They had been present at the siege of Jerusalem, Ezechiel 35.

32:30.text: Hunters of men, like Nemrod, the first king of Assyria.

32:32.text: My. Hebrew, Septuagint, "his," (Calmet) alluding to the ravages of Nechao; (Grotius) though the Hebrew may also signify my, as the Jews read Egypt, and Palestine, the land of the living, were filled with terror. After the latter had been chastised, Pharao might dread (Calmet) a worse fate; (Haydock) and the multitudes slain before him, might afford him some (Calmet) wretched consolation.
It is evident that these nations believed the existence of separate spirits, and had not given in to the errors of the Sadducees, or of the Metempsychosis.

33:2.text: Take. Before (chap. 3:17.) God made the choice, (Calmet) as he does here, ver. 7. He confirms the authority of those who are appointed to govern.
Meanest. Literally, "last," (Haydock) which seems to denote such as are chosen without regard to their dignity or obscurity in life, Genesis 47:2., and 3 Kings 12:31.
Pastors are not excused from admonishing the people for fear of danger or despair of reclaiming them, as each one is bound to do his duty.

33:6.text: Iniquity, not regarding the admonitions of his pastor; (chap. 3:18.; Calmet) or rather of conscience, when the guide proves faithless and silent, as in this instance.

The people were persuaded that none suffered except for some fault, ver. 10.; Jeremias 31:30., Daniel 13:52., and Genesis 44:16. The author of the Book of Job takes great pains to remove this mistake. God sometimes sends crosses for a trial, (Calmet) and to increase the merit of his servants; though it be very true, (Haydock) "no one is miserable except he deserve it."

33:8.text: Surely die a temporal, (Theodoret) or rather an eternal death.

33:10.text: Live? They suppose their case to be desperate, as their fathers had sinned, Ezechiel 18. The prophet shews that none are punished except for their own faults, (Calmet) and that "each one has free-will to be saved or to be lost."

33:11.text: Desire. The sinner's damnation is not an object of God's pleasure, Ezechiel 18:23.

33:11.src: St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologiae] 1:2. q. 109. and 112.; Worthington
33:11.text: He has an antecedent will to save all. He knocks at the door of our heart, (Apocalypse 3:20.) and if man do what depends on him, nothing will be wanting on the part of God.

33:12.text: Hurt him. God effaces all past crimes: yet a relapse makes them as it were revive, and is pardoned with more difficulty, Matthew 18:35. Some read, "In what day the converted sinner groans, he shall be saved," as if they had taken in part of Isaias 30:15.

33:17.text: Equitable; as we are much more inclined to vice than to virtue. This argumentation is inconclusive, as God owes nothing to man; and what good the latter does, is an effect of his grace. The propensity to evil is no excuse, as man is still free. He is judged according to the dispositions in which he is found at the hour of death; yet we must not infer, that those who have spent their lives in sinning will be no worse treated than the person who dies guilty of a single crime.

33:21.text: Year of the World 3417, Year before Christ 587.

Twelfth. Roman Septuagint, "tenth." Syriac, "eleventh year,...in the twelfth month;" which Theodoret thinks more probable, as the city was taken in the ninth of the fourth month of that year. Yet even so, it is strange that the news should not arrive before. Some think (Calmet) that the messenger came also to announce what happened after the death of Godolias.
Captivity. Here it is evident that the prophet dates from that event, Ezechiel 1., etc.
As he prophesied on the very day when the city was besieged, (chap. 24:2.) so (chap. 24:26.) he foretold that one should come three years after to inform him of the capture.
Then the people would believe him, (ibid. Ezechiel 24:27.) Calmet) and he would open his mouth boldly, ver. 22.

33:24.text: Places. He has answered those who despaired, ver. 10. Now he turns to the presumptuous, who expected to be treated like Abraham, though they did not imitate his virtues.

They perhaps entertained these sentiments before the death of Godolias, thinking to establish themselves in the land. Afterwards the prophet Jeremias could not prevail on them to remain, though God promised them security.

33:25.text: To them. Grabe marks to ver. 27., Thus saith, etc., as wanting in the Septuagint, though not in the Alexandrian copy. St. Jerome reckons eight verses or lines omitted.
The Complutensian and Theodoret read them with some variations.
The blood. It must be carefully extracted, Genesis 9:4., and Leviticus 7:26.
Uncleannesses; idols, in which you trust.

33:26.text: Swords, thinking to live thereby, (Genesis 27:40.) and to be secure. But I will disarm you. The pestilence shall find out those in the rocks, ver. 27.

33:30.text: Walls, the resort of idle people.

33:31.text: In to a religious meeting. (Chaldean) Perhaps they came on the sabbath to his house. Yet they made a just and song of his instructions.
They heard them with pleasure, but did not reform their lives.

33:33.text: Coming. The desolation of Jerusalem, (Haydock) and what I have foretold, hath already taken place; or, the news will presently arrive: as it did the following morning, ver. 21.

34:2.text: Shepherds. That is, princes, magistrates, chief priests, and scribes.
Shepherds may lawfully take milk, (1 Corinthians 9:7.) but the sheep and its wool belong to the master.
Excellent instructions are here given for all in authority.

34:3.text: Fat. Pastors often disguise the truth to flatter the rich, or the more just souls are ruined by their negligence.

34:4.text: Healed. God alone can restore to life. But pastors will not be excused by ignorance if they know not the maladies and the remedies of their flock.
Hand. This was blamed in the Pharisees, and is contrary to the spirit of the gospel, Matthew 23:4., and 1 Peter 5:2.

34:5.text: Field. The people being neglected, followed false prophets and idols. Their teachers were so far from striving to reclaim them, that they perhaps shewed them the example.

34:8.text: No shepherd. Pastors who seek only their temporal advantage, (1 Timothy 6:5., and Titus 1:7.; Haydock) are hirelings; and if they teach false doctrine, they are wolves, John 10.

34:10.text: Cease. Both the leaders and the people were led into captivity.

34:12.text: Day, in persecution. I will count my sheep, lest any be lost.

34:13.text: Land. All this cannot be understood of the synagogue alone.

34:16.text: Preserve, (Septuagint, Syriac) which seems more natural than Hebrew and Chaldean, "destroy." I will not eat them, like bad shepherds, ver. 3.
God and those whom he sends, will take care of the flock, Ephesians 4:

34:17.text: Cattle. The crimes of the pastors do not excuse the flock. In it there are people of different dispositions. The rich often destroy, and these are brought to an account, ver. 24.

34:23.text: David; Christ, who is of the house of David.
That king had been dead long before, so that no Jew or heretic can deny but that the Messias is here meant, as [in] Ezechiel 37:24., etc.

He possessed eminently all the virtues of David, and was of his seed. Zorobabel, etc., cannot be understood, as we have no proof that the governors after the captivity were chosen from the tribe of Juda, Ezechiel 21:27.

34:25.text: Peace. Christ pacifies all, Romans 15:33., Micheas 5:5., and Ephesians 6:15.
Beasts: those who promote idolatry; or, speaking of Christians, who teach heretical doctrine and persecute the Church.

34:26.text: Septuagint read not a blessing, which seems superfluous.
Yet Chaldean has it, and the idea is more complete.

34:29.text: A bud of renown, (germen nominatum.) He speaks of Christ, our Lord, the illustrious bud of the house of David, renowned over all the earth. See Jeremias 33:15.
Septuagint, Syriac, "a plant of peace." Chaldean, "established."

34:31.text: Men. What has been said of sheep (Menochius) relates to you.
Am the Lord. This is not in Hebrew or Protestants.
But it is found in one Hebrew manuscript and Septuagint, as this version is thus frequently confirmed.

35:2.text: Face. Address, or speak without fearing any man.
Seir: Idumea. He speaks of its devastation, both by Nabuchodonosor on the side of Moab, and by the Machabees in what remained, Jeremias 27:7., and 1 Machabees 4:60., and 14:33.

35:5.text: Enemy. God revenges the injuries done to his Church, as if they were done to himself, Acts 9:4.

The enmity of Jacob and of Esau seemed to commence in the womb, and was increased by the purchase of the birth-right, till Jacob's prudence disarmed his brother. Their descendants sought every opportunity to injure one another. The Idumeans shewed their ill-will particularly, when the Jews were most distressed. This was a piece of the greatest cruelty.

35:6.text: Blood. The Machabees sprung from the same stock. Pursue the Idumeans, and force them to receive circumcision, 1 Machabees 4:15.

35:9.text: Inhabited for a considerable time, as [in] Ezechiel 26:20.

35:10.text: Two. I will add the country of the Jews to my own, or will possess the land belonging to the kingdoms of Juda and of Israel. Their hopes were blasted. They never occupied any thing belonging to the Israelites, and what they had taken from Juda, as far as Hebron, was recovered.
There, witness of these designs, and guardian of his temple, etc.

35:11.text: Wrath. The Jews shall treat you as you have threatened to do unto them.
By them, my people. Septuagint, "to thee."

35:13.text: Derogated. Septuagint, "bawled;" or Hebrew, "multiplied your words against me," as if I could not protect my inheritance.
You would pretend to judge me, and to fathom my designs.

35:14.text: Rejoice. After the captivity, the Machabees shall conquer Edom.

35:15.text: As, etc., is not in the Roman Septuagint. St. Jerome says it was marked with an asterisk, being taken from Theodotion.
Grabe supplies to thou shalt. His copy has the rest:..."thou shalt know that I am their Lord."

36:1.text: Israel. The restoration of the Jews and the redemption of Christ, constitute this fourth division. (Worthington) -- Edom has seized parts of the country, as if I had abandoned my people for ever.

36:2.text: Heights. Septuagint, "deserts." The mountains are often styled eternal, as being the best symbols of durability, Deuteronomy 33:15.

36:3.text: Reproach. This God had repeatedly threatened, Deuteronomy 28:37., (Calmet) and Jeremias 24:9.

36:8.src: St. Augustine, Doct. 3:34.; St. Jerome; Menochius; Tirinus; Calmet
36:8.text: Israel. Some apply what follows to the captives returning; others, perceiving that the expressions were not then literally verified, have recourse to the reign of 1000 years. Others again explain the whole of Christ's Church. But some expressions refer to it, and others to the captives; which, though mixed together, induce no confusion, as what belongs to the Jews was a figure of what regarded Christ; and the predictions of the Messias might be applied to the Jews in an hyperbolical sense.

36:11.text: It would be difficult to shew that the Jews arrived at greater eminence after the captivity than before, (Calmet) though they were less addicted to idolatry. Their kingdom was not independent: they had no prophets, etc. But the superior numbers and excellence of the Christian Church is manifest.

36:12.src: St. Jerome; Eusebius, [History of the Church] 4:6.
36:12.text: Them. Antiochus and the Romans laid waste the country; (Calmet) and Adrian would not allow the Jews to come near Jerusalem.

The people were not indeed removed together, as they had been. But all this is verified only in the Church, which in the midst of persecutions always subsists. Theodoret thinks these promises were conditional with regard to the Jews.

36:13.text: Men. This remark was very ancient, Numbers 13:33. Wars had almost always raged in the country.

36:15.text: More. This whole promise principally relates to the Church of Christ, and God's perpetual protection of her; for as to the carnal Jews, they have been removed out of their land these sixteen hundred years.

36:18.text: Blood of their own children, and of the innocent, Ezechiel 16:36., and 22:2., and 33:25.

36:20.text: Land. Yet their conduct was no better than that of infidels. Thus they throw the blame on the law, and upon God himself, Jeremias 48:9., etc.
The Jews had provoked God to punish them with captivity; and hence the nations took occasion to blaspheme, that he could not protect them.

36:22.text: Holy, that my attributes of justice and mercy may be confessed, Isaias 48:2., Daniel 3:49., and Judith 8:24.

36:25.text: Water. R. David and the Chaldean explain this of the remission of sin; and all Christians understand it of baptism in water, remitting all offences, Ephesians 5:26., and Titus 3:5.
He alludes to the purification of the Jews, which prefigured baptism and penance, in which the blood of Christ is applied to our souls. This of course was only fulfilled in his church.

36:26.text: Flesh. The Jews at their return fell not so often into the sins of idolatry, etc., of which the prophets complained. But yet they were far from answering this character. Great irregularities prevailed under Nehemias, and in the days of the Machabees the priests publicly worshipped idols, 1 Esdras 9., and 2 Esdras 5., and 8., and 2 Machabees 4., and 5. Christ enables his servants to act with purity unto the end, by the influence of his all-powerful grace.

36:27.text: Do them. Hence the efficacy of grace appears, (St. Augustine; Haydock) and hereby some keep the commandments.

36:37.text: Find. Hebrew, "seek." I will cause great multitudes to come to the solemn feast. This was seen still more after the conversion of the Gentiles.

37:1.text: Spirit, by a divine impulse. He was not really removed.
It insinuates the liberation of the captives and of the Gentiles, as well as the mystery of the resurrection.
If this were not to take place, it would not be adduced as a figure.

37:5.text: Spirit. That is, life, soul, and breath.
At the resurrection, the same souls shall return to their respective bodies.

37:7.text: Noise of the bones moving, which denotes the wars of Cyrus and the hurry of the nations to which he restored liberty.

37:9.text: Spirit; wind or soul. The latter animates the body, as the grace (Calmet) of the Holy Spirit does the soul.

37:11.text: Off, like branches. Notwithstanding these desponding thoughts, God will restore his people, (Worthington) by means of Cyrus. The grave is often put for punishment and grievous calamities, Osee 6:3.

37:16.text: Associates. When Jeroboam caused the division, some of Israel adhered to Juda. The prophet glued the pieces of wood together, or engrafted them one into the other, to shew that all the people should be united, and the Jews and Gentiles concur to form one church.

Before the latter event took place God united the two kingdoms, to teach us that when we labour for the conversion of heretics, as in England, Catholics must first agree among themselves, that so all may more easily become one fold under one shepherd.

37:22.text: King. The Jews were long without kings, after the captivity; then they were not of the tribe of Juda, and all did not return. Christ fulfils the prediction.

37:23.text: Idols. The Jews, even many of the priests, worshipped them, under Antiochus.
But the Church is never defiled.

37:24.text: David; Christ, the one shepherd, (John x.) over all nations.
This cannot refer to Zorobabel, who was never king, nor perhaps even governor; as Sasabasar, who seems to have been a Persian, was appointed by Cyrus, 1 Esdras 1:8.
This title is more modest than that of king.

37:25.text: Ever. Let the Jews shew how this has been accomplished. We know that Christ preserves and rule his Church unto the end.

37:26.text: Peace. Can this regard the synagogue? Not a century passed without some flagrant misconduct, or chastisement. But our Saviour is the mediator of peace, Philippians 4:7.

38:2.text: Gog. This name, which signifies hidden, or covered, is taken in this place either for the persecutors of the Church of God in general, or some arch persecutor in particular; such as Antichrist shall be in the latter days. See Apocalypse 20:8. And what is said of the punishment of Gog, is verified by the unhappy end of persecutors.
Magog. Scythia or Tartary, whence the Turks and other enemies of the Church of Christ originally sprung.
These are supposed to be the princes of some northern and barbarous country, (Worthington) peopled by the son of Japhet.
St. Augustine (City of God 20:11.) observes, that as the Church is spread everywhere, so also are persecutors. But Christ will overcome them all.

This prophecy is very difficult. It seems literally to refer to Cambyses, who invaded Judea about seventy years after the temple had been destroyed, and perished by the hand of God, at Ecbatana, upon Mount Carmel. It is very usual with the prophets to give fictitious names; as the king of Tyre is called a cherub, and Jerusalem Ooliba. Cambyses ruled over Scythia, in Iberia; styled Gogarene, by Strabo (xi.).
The proper Scythia withstood all the attempts of his successors, and defeated Darius.

38:4.text: Jaws. Thus Cambyses treated the son of Psammenites. God has the most absolute dominion over all monarchs, ver. 7.

38:8.text: Cambyses invaded Egypt twelve years after the Jews had returned, and came upon them two years later, designing to plunder them, at the instigation of their malevolent neighbours. He had sustained great losses in Egypt, and was arrived at Carmel when news was brought that the false Smerdis had declared himself king. See Herodotus 3:61.

38:12.text: Midst. Literally, "navel." Hebrew, "Thabor," intimating a mountain rising in a plain, Judges 9:7.

Alluding to this intended blunder of the Jews lately returned, he speaks of Antichrist and of all heretics, who seek to pervert or suppress Catholics rescued from the power of the devil, by the sacraments of Christ.

38:13.text: Tharsis. Septuagint, "Carthage, and all their villages." The merchants or naval forces of Cambyses spurred him forward, (see Psalm 77:6.) as well as the Arabs, Cuthites, etc., 1 Esdras 4:8.

38:16.text: Latter. Antichrist shall persecute the Church at the end of the world.
After Cambyses had punished Egypt, his turn came, and all his designs were baffled.

38:17.text: Prophets. See Isaias xiv., and 16:21., and 26:1., and Jeremias 30:3., and 23., and Joel 2:30., and 3:1., etc.

38:19.text: Israel. The Jews shall be full of dismay, (ver. 20.) as the tyrant will vent some threats; (Calmet) or this commotion was in his own army.

38:21.text: Sword. The Jews were not in a condition to make resistance.

Cambyses had his brother Smerdis slain. But another was set on the throne; whereupon, mounting his horse in a fury, he wounded himself with his sword in the thigh, which mortifying, caused his death after twenty days.

The historian does not mention what became of his army; but it seems to have perished by discord, as was very common among such a collection of nations. See Judges 7:22., and Judith 15:1., and 1 Kings 14:20., and 4 Kings 2:21.
They were now without a head.
In the whole universe, God will at last destroy Antichrist and all his adherents.

38:22.text: Pestilence. His wound might be thus described.
Blood. Thus was he punished for shedding that of innocent people.

39:3.text: Arrows. Cambyses thought himself the most skilful at shooting. This was the usual weapon of the Persians (Calmet) and Parthians.

39:4.text: Fall. In every part some shall resist Antichrist, who shall be overpowered.

39:6.text: Islands; in the naval forces, or in the places of his dominion where discord shall prevail.

Cambyses resolved to attack Ethiopia, the people near the temple of Ammon, and the Carthaginians: but his troops refused to go against the latter, and many were smothered in the sands, or starved to death in the other expeditions.

39:9.text: Years; for a long time, ver. 12. Little fuel is necessary there. Mariana (Hist. 11:24. A.[The year of the Lord] 1212.) mentions, that the arms of the Moors supplied Spain four years with fuel.

39:10.text: Prey. The Jews relate that Zorobabel went out to oppose these nations, and took so much plunder as to be able to begin the temple.

39:11.text: East of the Mediterranean and of Carmel, the high road from Assyria to Egypt, (Genesis 37:2.; Calmet) where the Romans had a station at Legion.
Called, or shall be, Isaias 7:14., and 60:14.
Valley. Septuagint, "Gai, the grave of Gog." Protestants, "the valley of Haman-Gog," ver. 16.
Antichrist will not be burnt with material fire. But Catholics shall resist him zealously, and gain the victory.

39:12.text: Land, to prevent infection, and to comply with the law, Numbers 19:16.

39:13.text: Day. It does not occur in the calendar. Yet Comestor and [Denis] the Carthusian mentions Cambysa, the day when Bethulia was delivered, Judith 16:31. The history of Judith may perhaps relate to the defeat of this prince. The embellishments will not injure the truth. The Jews immediately after his death recommence the building of the temple, (1 Esdras iv.; Aggeus 1:14.) which they are forced to discontinue, till Hystaspes confirmed the decree of Cyrus. This was the grand object of their desires, and therefore the day when Cambyses fell was ever memorable to them.
So will that be for Christians, when Antichrist shall be no more.

39:15.text: Buriers. These might be foreign slaves, or Jews, who followed this employment.
They would be habitually (Haydock) unclean, and their company would be carefully avoided. Those who sought after the dead remains, contracted no uncleanness.

39:16.text: Septuagint, "a place for many graves:" poluandrion.
So many would be buried there, that the place might justly receive such a name; though we find no such city in the neighbourhood.
Rabbath, of Ammon, was sometimes so called.

39:18.text: That are. Hebrew, "and of meries, (a species of oxen, in Syria.; Bellon. 2:50.) all of them from Basan," beyond the Jordan, a most fertile country. Septuagint, "all calves fattened."

39:23.text: Sword. I could as easily have protected them against the Chaldeans as against this prince, if their sins had not been in the way.
These bring on the ruin of God's people more than the power of the enemy.

39:25.text: Israel. All who please may now return. Hystaspes was very favourable to them. He married Esther, raised Mardochai to great power, and sent Esdras into Judea with ample privileges. Many returned under him, Artazerxes, and Alexander [the Great].
Jealous. I will restore the people to favour, and will protect them (Calmet) like a husband.

39:28.text: There, except such as refused to return. They have entire liberty. This and what follows was not fully accomplished till the propagation of the gospel. God wished all to embrace it; and though Israel has been partly blind, they will enter the Church after the Gentiles, Romans 12:26.

40:1.text: Year of the World 3430, Year before Christ 574.
Month; April 30, the year of the world 3430.

In explaining this last most obscure vision: 1:The Jews say it was verified after the captivity. But thus the temple would be four miles round, and the city thirty-six, which never was the case. 2:Hence more modern Jews assert it will be fulfilled by the Messias. 3:Rejecting these errors, Lyranus, etc., suppose that the promise was conditional, and that the sins of the Jews prevented the city and temple from being so large; and that the mystical temple of the Church is also insinuated, into which both Jews and Gentiles shall enter. 4:Though this opinion be probable, it seems better to follow St. Jerome, St. Gregory, etc., who cannot apply all to the Old Testament, nor think that such a huge temple and city were indeed promised, but explain some parts of the captives at their return, as a figure of those redeemed by Christ, and brought into his Church, which is adorned with all graces. Yet the greatest part must be understood of the Church triumphant.

St. Jerome confesses his ignorance of this subject; and those who have come after him, though they imitate not his modesty, add little to dissipate the mist with which this vision is surrounded. Some have adhered too close to Josephus, while Villalpand has here discovered all the magnificence of Greek and Roman architecture, and has represented the temple six times as large as it really was. It seems that the prophet has described the same temple of Solomon which he had seen, that the dimensions might be preserved, and the hopes of the people kept up; (Calmet) and that they might comprehend what a loss they actually sustained on account of their sins, (Haydock) and might strive to come up to this pattern (Du Hamel) as "near as they should be able," the wealth of the people being much less than Solomon's.

These arguments do not, however, shew that the buildings were to resemble each other. They vary in many particulars; and the prophet would specify what was to be really executed. He says nothing of the ornamental part, and little of the height, which are the most expensive.
If some things appear to be too grand for the temple of Solomon, and of Zorobabel, we must reflect that the prophet passes from the figure to the Church of Christ, which is not unusual.
Alcasar and Bossuet explain it wholly of the Church, so that the letter requires few notes.

40:2.text: Mountain; Moria, in spirit. On the eastern side, the road to the city was level. Walls were added after the captivity, on the other sides.
City, the temple was so large.
South, to one coming from the north, though the temple lay on that side of the town, Psalm 47:2.
Septuagint read mongod, "over-against," instead of mongob, "south," which has perplexed many.
Ezechiel was placed on Sion, which was not very high, but here it denotes the Church. Jerusalem was in ruins.

40:3.text: This angel waited for the prophet at the northern gate, but introduced him by that looking towards the east, ver. 16.
Line. It seems never to have been used.

40:5.text: Breadth. Hebrew, Chaldean, Septuagint, "of six cubits, in a cubit and a palm;" intimating that the sacred cubit contained six palms, while the common one had only five, (chap. 43:13.; Worthington) being half a yard; (Arbuthnot) or the Hebrew cubit was a hand's breadth larger than the Babylonian, or about 21.5 inches, (Calmet) which may be styled (Menochius) the sacred cubit.

Reed. This outer wall (Calmet; Menochius) was to prevent any from falling down the precipice. It was about four yards nine inches in height and thickness, being so solid in order that the ground might not give way. Josephus describes prodigious walls, (Haydock) reaching to the bottom of the mountain, three hundred cubits on the south and west; but then the temple was much enlarged.

40:6.text: And. In this verse occurs the first of thirty-four words where the j is allowed by the keri to be omitted in this one chapter, always when it is the sign of the plural number before a suffixed v, and of course by voluntary assimilation. But Camb. Manuscript has the j regularly in the thirty-two of these words.
Steps. The ground was not quite level, which caused the buildings to rise one above another more elegantly. There were four great gates.

40:7.text: Chamber, for the porters, (Calmet) three on each side of the porch, ver. 10.

40:14.text: Cubits high, or else the elevation is nowhere specified.

40:16.text: They were larger within, to afford light, as in Solomon's temple, and in castles. Interpreters disagree in their sentiments: but all allow that God here insinuated that he would reward people in the Old Testament with temporal, and those in the New with spiritual blessings; first with grace in this life, and with glory in the next.
Yet we must not suppose that the saints of old were deprived of eternal goods.
About. The other gates had the like ornaments.

40:17.text: There were chambers. Gazophylacia, so called, because the priests and Levites kept in them the stores and vessels that belonged to the temple.

They went all round the courts. The women were in the second story. Villalpand and Capel, who are deemed the most accurate, suppose that there were no chambers below, but an open gallery. There seem, however, to have been chambers also, Ezechiel 8:7.

40:18.text: Lower. So that there was a step up to the threshold.
Marble was used in all places exposed to the weather.

40:26.text: Seven. There were eight to the inner court, (ver. 31.) shewing that more perfection is now required, and a greater reward given.

40:30.text: Five cubits. The parallel passages would intimate "fifty."
But the texts all read five, and Maldonat understands the four vacant spaces between the chambers, ver. 7.

40:38.text: Gates. Hebrew is very obscure. The holocausts were slain on the north side of the altar, Leviticus 1:11.

40:43.text: Borders, to prevent any thing falling. Chaldean, etc., have "hooks."

40:44.text: North. The singers occupied two wings.

40:45.text: Watch over the sacred ornaments, perfumes, lamps of the sanctuary, etc., Numbers 3:28.

40:46.text: Altar. Not on actual service, (chap. 41:13.) but to prepare the victims.

40:48.text: That side. Septuagint add, "and the opening (euros, width) of the gate was 14 cubits, and the breadth (projections, epomides) of the," etc., (Haydock) or the wall on each side of the door was three cubits, so that the porch was 20 cubits broad.

40:49.text: Eleven. 3 Kings vi., says ten. The exact breadth was ten and a half, (Villalpand; Tirinus) or what the pavement covered is there specified according to some. Roman Septuagint has "twelve." But the edition of Basil reads more correctly, ten.
Yet both ten and eleven may be right, if this temple be different.
Eight. Hebrew seems corrupt, a being substituted for a. "They mounted by ten steps," as the Septuagint read. Aquila has eleven; Symmachus [has] eight.
As they are not measured, they were like Solomon's.

41:1.text: The temple. This plan of a temple, which was here shewn to the prophet in a vision, partly had relation to the material temple, which was to be rebuilt: and partly, in a mystical sense, to the spiritual temple of God, the Church of Christ.

41:1.src: St. Jerome; St. Augustine, City of God 18:45.; Worthington
41:1.text: The description seems grander than Solomon's; and yet Zorobabel's temple was meaner than his. Hence this prophecy, and those of Aggeus and Zacharias, cannot be understood of it, but of the Church.

41:3.text: Front, or door-posts. The temple was 20 cubits broad.

41:5.text: Four. Herein it differed from Solomon's chambers, which were in breadth five, six, and seven cubits, in the three stories, respectively.

41:6.text: One by another, or one over another: literally, side to side, or side upon side.

The word side is here equivocal, like Hebrew tsela, sometimes denoting the boards, and at other times the apartments round the temple, except on the west, over against the sanctuary, which seems to be the case here. It is true, we shall not easily find sixty-six chambers. But the Hebrew, etc., have only thirty-three.

Villalpand rightly translated anterides, (ver. 5.) but here he substitutes trabes, "beams or thick planks," to make it agree with 3 Kings 6:6., as if the same temple were meant. He also adopts the Vulgate bis.
Twice. Hebrew pehamim. Yet he afterwards places in his commentary, pedes, "thirty-three feet, that is twenty-two cubits," though feet are never used at all as a measure in Scripture.

41:7.text: Broader, as the wall was not so thick.
Midst. The two staircases were round in the hollow of the wall, (Menochius) at the eastern end of the chambers. (Josephus) See 3 Kings.

41:9.text: House. Because these side-chambers were in the very walls of the temple all around: or it may also be rendered, (more agreeably to the Hebrew) so as to signify, that the thickness of the wall for the side-chambers within was the same as that of the wall without; that is, equally five cubits.
Protestants, "and that which was left, was the place of the side-chambers that were within."

To manifest a greater respect for God, the apartments of the priests were twenty-five cubits distant from the temple, (ver. 10.) and divided by a wall, which Josephus describes as three cubits high. (Antiquities 8:2.) This wall was at the distance of five cubits from the temple.

41:11.text: Prayer, or "separated;" munnach, (Haydock) the inner house, ver. 9.

41:13.text: Separate, different from that [in] ver. 9., (Calmet; Hebrew gizra.; Haydock) between the temple and the priests' apartments, which it may also designate, (Calmet) unless it (Haydock) mean the temple itself, which was divided from the rest, (Menochius) and set aside for prayer, etc.

41:15.text: Galleries; Ethecas, or as the more correct manuscripts of St. Jerome read, Ecthetas, seems to be formed from (Calmet) Hebrew athikim, (Haydock) which St. Jerome explains a balcony, portico, or gallery.

41:16.text: About. Thus he intimates the inner temple, which was covered with boards.

Windows. The temple was hidden so far by the chambers; or rather he measured (Calmet) "the ground and up to the windows, which were" shut up with lattices, affording light over the doors (Haydock) and apartments, fifteen cubits from the ground.

41:18.text: Two. Before he describes them with four faces. But they varied, (Exodus 25:18.) or the other two might be against the wall.

The cherub signified "knowledge," and the palm-tree victory, to shew that man must be instructed with divine knowledge, and so fight for the victory. The face of man implied that Christ would suffer, and that of a lion announced that he would overcome all his enemies.

41:21.text: The threshold was four-square. That is, the gate of the temple was four-square, and so placed as to answer the gate of the sanctuary within.
It was not an arch, though not a perfect square, 3 Kings 6:34.

41:22.text: Table, or altar of incense, larger than that of Moses, Exodus 30:1.

41:23.text: Sanctuary. One folding door opened into it, the other into the temple.

41:24.text: Other. It was not perhaps necessary to open the large door.

41:26.text: Sides, (humerulis) or pilasters. Hebrew, "on the shoulder of the porch, and on the sides of the house, and beams."

42:1.text: And. St. Jerome finding the Septuagint and others varying in the explanations, gives his own; yet adopts the saying of Socrates, "I know that I am ignorant:" for this also is a part of science.
Outward, with respect to the temple. It is styled inner court, with reference to that of the people.

42:3.text: Triple, one over another, (ver. 6.) or on a level with the priests' court.

42:4.text: One cubit, projecting out perhaps for seats, or for the pilasters.

42:5.text: Above. The higher stories are not so lofty.

42:6.text: Fifty cubits. We only allow thirty, as this is not in Hebrew, etc. The text, ver. 10., and 12., seems incorrect.

42:13.text: Holies. Hebrew, "who are very holy," and (Haydock) on duty. See Leviticus 6:25.

42:14.text: People. Ezechiel 44:17., and Exodus 33:42. Christian priests use their vestments only when they are officiating.

42:16.text: Five. Hebrew emoth should be meoth, "three hundred, as it is printed (yet not in the Protestant version. Haydock) thrice just after, and as it is written here in Manuscript 4. Camb. 1. 2."
Reeds. Septuagint, "cubits," as Hebrew, St. Jerome, etc., insinuate. The mountain was only five hundred paces square, even when it was enlarged in the time of Josephus.
If we retain reeds, (Haydock) the wall will be twelve thousand cubits, (Menochius) which seems improbable. (Tournemine) See Ezechiel 45.

42:20.text: Cubits. This confirms the former correction, ver. 16.
Hebrew has only, "of five hundred." Maldonat and Cornelius a Lapide would understand reeds, to agree with what goes before.
Separation. Josephus mentions a wall three cubits high, on the pillars of which was written in Greek and Latin, that such as entered the temple must be pure, (Calmet) and no stranger.
Into this court all might come.

43:2.text: Majesty. The world is enlightened by the preaching of the gospel; and the Church triumphant shall shine in perfection, when that which is sown in corruption shall put on incorruption, 1 Corinthians xv.

43:2.src: St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologiae] p. 3. q. 27. a. 3.; Worthington
43:2.text: The blessed Virgin [Mary] conceiving Jesus Christ may be insinuated.

43:3.text: He came. Hebrew, "I came to destroy (Septuagint, to anoint) the city," marking such as should be spared, Ezechiel 9. The glorious chariot entered by the eastern gate, and the prophet followed to the court of the priests, ver. 5, 13.

The Jews say the second temple was deprived of the Shekinah, or glory of the Lord. Yet it seems here to enter; and Christ himself adorned this second house, more than the first, by his adorable presence, Aggeus 2:8.

43:7.text: Said. The Lord spoke, (Chaldean; Theodoret) or the angel, (St. Jerome) in human shape.
He addresses the prophet, ver. 12 to Ezechiel 44:5., though what follows immediately seems to belong to the Lord, (Calmet) in whose name he speaks.

Name. God hath abandoned the synagogue, but will remain with his Church unto the end, Matthew 23:38., and 28:20. Yet the perfect Church, without spot, is the [Church] triumphant [in heaven]; (Ephesians 5:27.; Worthington) though the Catholic Church, on earth, is ever holy and "the communion of saints."

Carcasses. Idols, according to some; or rather by the kings being buried on Sion. This is nowhere else reprehended; neither is their building too near the temple, which the prophet here condemns, ver. 8.

It seems, however, that if these things had been blameable, such a number of pious and wise kings would not have acted thus, nor the prophets have neglected to admonish them of their duty. The carcasses and houses here specified may have been vestiges of idolatry; or, in future, the tombs and palaces were to be at a more respectful distance.

43:8.text: Wall. The kings of Juda had a door communicating with the temple, by which they entered on the west. It was guarded by Levites, 1 Paralipomenon 26:16. Ezechiel places no door on that side. Yet in Herod's temple we find one leading to the adjacent palace, and three others into the town.

43:9.text: The remains of the dead were probably not disturbed; but no more, that we know of, were placed on Sion.
Kings may signify idols, Moloc, etc., which they had worshipped, and which some had even placed in the holy place, to the nation's ruin.

43:10.text: Measure, that they may be convinced of their ingratitude, (Menochius) which has deprived them of so noble a structure, and put them under the necessity of beginning so great a work again. It required all the exertions of the prophets to make them go forward with it, Aggeus 1.

43:11.text: In the whole fabric, (Menochius) as thou hast described it, (Haydock) or received from the angel, with all the ceremonies to be observed.

43:12.text: Border. Nothing but the temple shall be on this mountain. It shall be wholly consecrated to the Lord. This was ill observed. The Asmonean princes erected the famous tower Antonia, at the north side. (Josephus) See ver. 8. The Jews assert that it was unlawful to spit on this ground, etc., but no such thing is specified in Josephus or in Scripture, only we find that none were to go out by the same door at which they had entered, (chap. 46:9.; Calmet) except the king.

43:13.text: By. Hebrew, "by cubits." This cubit is a common cubit, etc. The Babylonian, (Haydock) or sacred one, was a palm longer.
Breadth. It was the same as the depth, being designed to convey the blood by a conduit to the torrent Cedron.
Trench, or bottom aforesaid. Septuagint, "the height."
Chaldean, "disposition of the altar," which seems best.

43:14.text: Cubit. They were each a cubit broad, but this greater means higher up. Some assert that the priests stood on this base to avoid treading on the altar, when they placed the wood or victims. But it would be too low; and steps were made for that purpose. The altar was ten, or rather twelve cubits high, and as many broad. Solomon's was ten high and twenty broad. Herod's was a square of forty cubits, raised fifteen from the ground.

43:15.text: The ariel. That is, the altar itself, or rather the highest part of it, upon which the burnt-offerings were laid. In the Hebrew it is harel, that is, the mountain of God; but in the following verse haariel, that is, the lion of God; a figure, from its consuming, and as it were devouring the sacrifices as a lion devours its prey.

It also appeared like a little mountain in the court. See Isaias 29:1. The altar was probably made of brass, like Solomon's. Josephus and Philo say that rough stones were used after the captivity: but it seems little attention was paid to the dimensions of the temple, etc., given by Ezechiel.
Yet the prophets were present to see the laws of God executed, and never complain of their infringement in these particulars, which were attended with no great difficulty, Ezechiel 40:1.
It is certain the Jews never thought themselves bound to make their altars of a determinate size.

43:17.text: Crown, which was usually placed round altars and tables, that nothing might fall down.
Bottom of the altar, or base, mentioned [in] ver. 13.
Steps, boarded on each side, Exodus 20:26.

43:19.text: Levites. Hebrew, "of the race of Levi, of the family of Sadoc." He had obtained the dignity under Saul, (Calmet) and after the deposition of Abiathar, under Solomon, (3 Kings 2:26.; Haydock) his posterity always preserved it.
Calf. Thou shalt give it, or make known these rites to the priests.
People are often said to do what they only command, as [in] ver. 20, etc.

43:21.text: Sanctuary, out of the precincts of the temple, but on the mountain, Leviticus 4:3., and 6:11.

43:22.text: He-goat. Septuagint add, "of the goats," (hircum caprarum. Haydock) denoting a young kid, (Menochius) or one selected (Haydock) rather from the flock.

43:26.text: Consecrate it. Literally, "fill his hand;" that is, dedicate and apply it to holy service, (Challoner) as the Hebrew, etc., speak of the altar. Septuagint, "they shall fill their hands," seems more natural, (Exodus 28:41.) and is adopted by Pagnin, Vatable, etc.

44:2.text: Opened. No man perfectly understands the Scriptures but the Son of God, Matthew 11:27.
This also insinuates, that Mary ever remained a pure virgin.

Shut, even for the prince. Some (Haydock) kings of Juda claimed the privilege of entering by it at any time, and passed through a part of the court assigned to the priests, 2 Paralipomenon 6:12., and 4 Kings 16:18. This shall be no longer tolerated. The gate might be opened in the week days if the prince wished to offer sacrifice, but not else, except on the sabbaths, Ezechiel 46:1, 12.
Lord, in peace offerings and religious feasts.
Porch. Each had two doors, Ezechiel 46:2.

44:3.text: Opened. No man perfectly understands the Scriptures but the Son of God, Matthew 11:27.
This also insinuates, that Mary ever remained a pure virgin.

Shut, even for the prince. Some (Haydock) kings of Juda claimed the privilege of entering by it at any time, and passed through a part of the court assigned to the priests, 2 Paralipomenon 6:12., and 4 Kings 16:18. This shall be no longer tolerated. The gate might be opened in the week days if the prince wished to offer sacrifice, but not else, except on the sabbaths, Ezechiel 46:1, 12.
Lord, in peace offerings and religious feasts.
Porch. Each had two doors, Ezechiel 46:2.

44:4.text: Face. This astonishment and attention (ver. 5.) intimate the mysteries of the Church, prefigured by the Jewish rites.

44:5.text: Ways, or "the going in," and all that concerns the temple and sacrifices.

44:7.text: Flesh. People of this description were not to enter the temple, which is here called the sanctuary, even though they adored God. In some cases pagans were admitted into any part, except the most holy. Epiphanes (Calmet) and Pompey forced their passage thither, to the great sorrow of the Jews. (Josephus, Jewish Wars 1:5.) See Ecclesiasticus lii., and Acts 21:28.
During the plundering of the city, the Chaldeans did the like; (Lamentations 1:10.; Haydock) and while idols were set up, all rules were neglected.
Doings. You observe the exterior, and not the spirit of religion.

44:8.text: Yourselves, out of ambition, contrary to the regulations of David.

44:10.text: Levites, or priests, (Haydock) who were seduced by Jeroboam, or offered victims on the high places, 4 Kings 23:9. Such lost their privileges and were condemned to serve in the meanest offices. It seems the race of Ithamar was alone guilty, and the threat against his house was now put in execution, 1 Kings 2:39., and 3:14., and Leviticus 21:17., and 2 Esdras 7:64.

44:11.text: Slay. Levites might do this, but the priests poured the blood round the altar, ver. 15. They could not well slay all the victims.

44:15.text: And Levites, of the higher order.
No simple Levites were of this family. It enjoyed the high priesthood form the time of Solomon.

44:17.text: Court; that is to do duty.
Linen. Wool might have been taken from a dead sheep, which would render the person unclean.

44:18.text: Sweat, or so as to make them perspire. Septuagint, "girded violently."
They must have liberty to move, (St. Jerome) and have the girdle about their breast.

44:19.text: Sanctify, by exposing them to the danger of touching the sacred vestments, which none were to touch but they that were sanctified.
Sanctify here means to defile, Exodus 30:29., (Calmet) and 29:37. Holy things must be done in holy places.
